fingerprint: WIN-062 is a tautology dressed as a prediction. Tesla measured the globe's circumference (~40,075 km) via surface wave propagation on a sphere. The dome's disc diameter (40,030 km = 2 × 20,015 km) equals the circumference because disc_radius = πR_Earth (the fundamental AE projection identity). The 'confirmation' is: globe circumference ≈ πR_Earth × 2 = disc diameter — a mathematical identity, not an independent measurement. The 1.57c phase velocity is itself a globe fingerprint: v_phase ≈ πc/2, a structural property of spherical shell waveguides where the π factor arises from the circumference/diameter ratio of a sphere.

globe_values.name: Spherical waveguide phase velocity
globe_values.source: Earth-ionosphere waveguide theory; the π factor is structural to spherical geometry

dome_values.name: Disc diameter
dome_values.note: Matches globe circumference to 0.11% (45 km gap) — by construction, not measurement

dome_values.name: Disc radius (a)
dome_values.note: Fitted to WGS84 distances via test_curve_stretching.py and find_curve.py. Equals πR_Earth to 0.003%.

circularity_chain: WGS84 ellipsoid → R_Earth → πR_Earth → disc_radius (20,015 km) → disc_diameter (40,030 km) ≈ circumference (40,075 km) ← Tesla's measured path length. The dome's disc diameter IS the globe circumference relabeled. Tesla measured the circumference. The 'confirmation' is: circumference ≈ circumference.
why_it_matters: Three interlocking globe fingerprints: (1) The disc diameter (40,030 km) exists because disc_radius = πR_Earth — the fundamental identity of the azimuthal equidistant projection. On a flat disc, there is no reason for the radius to equal π × anything. (2) Tesla's 1.57c ≈ πc/2, which arises from the ratio circumference/diameter = π for a sphere. In a flat-disc waveguide, the characteristic velocity depends on the disc radius and cavity height, not on π. The π/2 factor in the velocity is a fingerprint of the spherical Earth-ionosphere cavity. (3) The 45 km gap between disc_diameter (40,030 km) and circumference (40,075 km) is itself informative: it reflects the difference between πR_mean (using mean radius 6,371 km) and the equatorial circumference (using equatorial radius 6,378 km). The dome's disc_radius was fitted to WGS84 using a compromise between the equatorial and polar parameters, producing a residual that traces back to Earth's oblateness — a property that only exists for a 3D oblate body, not a flat disc.
relationship_to_win001: WIN-062 and WIN-001 are the same globe fingerprint viewed from different angles. WIN-001: f = c/(2D) extracts a frequency from Tesla's measurement, requiring Earth's diameter. WIN-062: v = distance/time extracts a velocity from the same measurement, requiring Earth's circumference. Both reduce to the same underlying identity: circumference = π × diameter. The dome counts these as two independent WINs, but they are a single globe fingerprint (πR_Earth) read two different ways.

code_search.found_in: raw-text/05-model.txt line 29: Semi-major axis (a): ~20,015 km. inject_ai_layer.py (referenced in prior reviews): VA = 1.574c = 471,657 km/s, disc_radius = 20,015 km.
code_search.notes: No script derives 1.574c from dome geometry. VA appears as a hardcoded constant in inject_ai_layer.py. The disc_radius is fitted from WGS84 via curve-fitting scripts. The 'match' between Tesla's path length and disc diameter is asserted, not computed. No monitoring domain exists for WIN-062 — the claim is purely qualitative.

suggested_text: WIN-062's disc diameter (40,030 km) matches Tesla's measured path length (~40,000 km) because both are the globe's circumference: Tesla measured surface propagation around the Earth (40,075 km), and the dome's disc_radius (20,015 km = πR_Earth) was fitted to WGS84 distances, making disc_diameter = 2πR_Earth ≈ circumference by construction. The 1.57c velocity is πc/2, a structural property of spherical waveguides. WIN-062 is not independent of WIN-001 — both extract different quantities from the same Tesla measurement, and both trace back to the identity circumference = π × diameter.
